title: The Harp-Player On Etna
content:
آ آ آ آ I

آ آ آ آ THE LAST GLEN

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Hist! once more!
آ آ آ آ Listen, Pausanias! Aye, ï؟½tis Callicles!
آ آ آ آ I know those notes among a thousand.آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Hark!

آ آ آ آ CALLICLES

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ (Sings unseen, from below.)
آ آ آ آ The track winds down to the clear stream,
آ آ آ آ To cross the sparkling shallows; there
آ آ آ آ The, cattle love to gather, on their way
آ آ آ آ To the high mountain pastures, and to stay,
آ آ آ آ Till the rough cow-herds drive them past,
آ آ آ آ Knee-deep in the cool ford; for ï؟½tis the last
آ آ آ آ Of all the woody, high, well-waterï؟½d dells
آ آ آ آ On Etna; and the beam
آ آ آ آ Of noon is broken there by chestnut boughs
آ آ آ آ Down its steep verdant sides; the air
آ آ آ آ Is freshenï؟½d by the leaping stream, which throws
آ آ آ آ Eternal showers of spray on the mossï؟½d roots
آ آ آ آ Of trees, and veins of turf, and long dark shoots
آ آ آ آ Of ivy-plants, and fragrant hanging bells
آ آ آ آ Of hyacinths, and on late anemonies,
آ آ آ آ That muffle its wet banks; but glade,
آ آ آ آ And stream, and sward, and chestnut trees,
آ آ آ آ End here; Etna beyond, in the broad glare
آ آ آ آ Of the hot noon, without a shade,
آ آ آ آ Slope behind slope, up to the peak, lies bare;
آ آ آ آ The peak, round which the white clouds play.
آ آ آ آ In such a glen, on such a day,
آ آ آ آ On Pelion, on the grassy ground,
آ آ آ آ Chiron, the aged Centaur, lay,
آ آ آ آ The young Achilles standing by.
آ آ آ آ The Centaur taught him to explore
آ آ آ آ The mountains; where the glens are dry,
آ آ آ آ And the tired Centaurs come to rest,
آ آ آ آ And where the soaking springs abound,
آ آ آ آ And the straight ashes grow for spears,
آ آ آ آ And where the hill-goats come to feed,
آ آ آ آ And the sea-eagles build their nest.
آ آ آ آ He showï؟½d him Phthia far away,
آ آ آ آ And said: O boy, I taught this lore
آ آ آ آ To Peleus, in long distant years!
آ آ آ آ He told him of the Gods, the stars,
آ آ آ آ The tides;, and then of mortal wars,
آ آ آ آ And of the life which heroes lead
آ آ آ آ Before they reach the Elysian place
آ آ آ آ And rest in the immortal mead;
آ آ آ آ And all the wisdom of his race.



آ آ آ آ II

آ آ آ آ TYPHO

آ آ آ آ [He advances to the edge of the crater. Smoke
آ آ آ آ and fire break forth with a loud noise, and
آ آ آ آ CALLICLES is heard below singing:

آ آ آ آ The lyreï؟½s voice is lovely everywhere!
آ آ آ آ In the court of Gods, in the city of men,
آ آ آ آ And in the lonely rock-strewn mountain glen.
آ آ آ آ In the still mountain air.

آ آ آ آ Only to Typho it sounds hatefully!
آ آ آ آ To Typho only, the rebel oï؟½erthrown,
آ آ آ آ Through whose heart Etna drives her roots of stone,
آ آ آ آ To imbed them in the sea.

آ آ آ آ Wherefore dost thou groan so loud?
آ آ آ آ Wherefore do thy nostrils flash,
آ آ آ آ Through the dark night, suddenly,
آ آ آ آ Typho, such red jets of flame?
آ آ آ آ Is thy torturï؟½d heart still proud?
آ آ آ آ Is thy fire-scathï؟½d arm still rash?
آ آ آ آ Still alert thy stone-crushï؟½d frame?
آ آ آ آ Doth thy fierce soul still deplore
آ آ آ آ The ancient rout by the Cilician hills,
آ آ آ آ And that curst treachery on the Mount of Gore?
آ آ آ آ Do thy bloodshot eyes still see
آ آ آ آ The fight that crownï؟½d thy ills,
آ آ آ آ Thy last defeat in this Sicilian sea?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou sworn, in thy sad lair,
آ آ آ آ Where east the strong sea-currents suckï؟½d thee down,
آ آ آ آ Never to cease to writhe, and try to sleep,
آ آ آ آ Letting the sea-stream wander through thy hair?
آ آ آ آ That thy groans, like thunder deep,
آ آ آ آ Begin to roll, and almost drown
آ آ آ آ The sweet notes, whose lulling spell
آ آ آ آ Gods and the race of mortals love so well,
آ آ آ آ When through thy eaves thou hearest music swell?

آ آ آ آ But an awful pleasure bland
آ آ آ آ Spreading oï؟½er the Thundererï؟½s face,
آ آ آ آ When the sound climbs near his seat,
آ آ آ آ The Olympian council sees;
آ آ آ آ As he lets his lax right hand,
آ آ آ آ Which the lightnings doth embrace,
آ آ آ آ Sink upon his mighty knees.
آ آ آ آ And the eagle, at the beck
آ آ آ آ Of the appeasing gracious harmony,
آ آ آ آ Droops all his sheeny, brown, deep-featherï؟½d neck,
آ آ آ آ Nestling nearer to Joveï؟½s feet;
آ آ آ آ While oï؟½er his sovereign eye
آ آ آ آ The curtains of the blue films slowly meet,
آ آ آ آ And the white Olympus peaks
آ آ آ آ Rosily brighten, and the soothï؟½d Gods smile
آ آ آ آ At one another from their golden chairs,
آ آ آ آ And no one round the charmï؟½d circle speaks.
آ آ آ آ Only the loved Hebe bears
آ آ آ آ The cup about, whose draughts beguile
آ آ آ آ Pain and care, with a dark store
آ آ آ آ Of fresh-pullï؟½d violets wreathï؟½d and nodding oï؟½er;
آ آ آ آ And her flushï؟½d feet glow on the marble floor.



آ آ آ آ III

آ آ آ آ MARSYAS

آ آ آ آ CALLICLES (from below)

آ آ آ آ As the sky-brightening south-wind clears the day,
آ آ آ آ And makes the massï؟½d clouds roll,
آ آ آ آ The music of the lyre blows away
آ آ آ آ The clouds that wrap the soul.

آ آ آ آ Oh, that Fate had let me see
آ آ آ آ That triumph of the sweet persuasive lyre!
آ آ آ آ That famous, final victory
آ آ آ آ When jealous Pan with Marsyas did conspire!

آ آ آ آ When, from far Parnassusï؟½ side,
آ آ آ آ Young Apollo, all the pride
آ آ آ آ Of the Phrygian flutes to tame,
آ آ آ آ To the Phrygian highlands came!
آ آ آ آ Where the long green reed-beds sway
آ آ آ آ In the rippled waters grey
آ آ آ آ Of that solitary lake
آ آ آ آ Where Maeanderï؟½s springs are born;
آ آ آ آ Where the ridgï؟½d pine-wooded roots
آ آ آ آ Of Messogis westward break,
آ آ آ آ Mounting westward, high and higher.
آ آ آ آ There was held the famous strife;
آ آ آ آ There the Phrygian brought his flutes,
آ آ آ آ And Apollo brought his lyre;
آ آ آ آ And, when now the westering sun
آ آ آ آ Touchï؟½d the hills, the strife was done,
آ آ آ آ And the attentive Muses said
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Marsyas! thou art vanquishï؟½d.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then Apolloï؟½s minister
آ آ آ آ Hangï؟½d upon a branching fir
آ آ آ آ Marsyas, that unhappy Faun,
آ آ آ آ And began to whet his knife.
آ آ آ آ But the Maenads, who were there,
آ آ آ آ Left their friend, and with robes flowing
آ آ آ آ In the wind, and loose dark hair
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er their polishï؟½d bosoms blowing,
آ آ آ آ Each her ribbonï؟½d tambourine
آ آ آ آ Flinging on the mountain sod,
آ آ آ آ With a lovely frightenï؟½d mien
آ آ آ آ Came about the youthful God.
آ آ آ آ But he turnï؟½d his beauteous face
آ آ آ آ Haughtily another way,
آ آ آ آ From the grassy sun-warmï؟½d place,
آ آ آ آ Where in proud repose he lay,
آ آ آ آ With one arm over his head,
آ آ آ آ Watching how the whetting sped.

آ آ آ آ But aloof on the lake strand,
آ آ آ آ Did the young Olympus stand,
آ آ آ آ Weeping at his masterï؟½s end;
آ آ آ آ For the Faun had been his friend.
آ آ آ آ For he taught him how to sing.
آ آ آ آ And he taught him flute-playing.
آ آ آ آ Many a morning had they gone
آ آ آ آ To the glimmering mountain lakes,
آ آ آ آ And had torn up by the roots
آ آ آ آ The tall crested water-reeds
آ آ آ آ With long plumes, and soft brown seeds,
آ آ آ آ And had carved them into flutes,
آ آ آ آ Sitting on a tabled stone
آ آ آ آ Where the shoreward ripple breaks.
آ آ آ آ And he taught him how to please
آ آ آ آ The red-snooded Phrygian girls,
آ آ آ آ Whom the summer evening sees
آ آ آ آ Flashing in the danceï؟½s whirls
آ آ آ آ Underneath the starlit trees
آ آ آ آ In the mountain villages.
آ آ آ آ Therefore now Olympus stands,
آ آ آ آ At his masterï؟½s piteous cries
آ آ آ آ Pressing fast with both his hands
آ آ آ آ His white garment to his eyes,
آ آ آ آ Not to see Apolloï؟½s scorn;
آ آ آ آ Ah, poor Faun, poor Faun! ah, poor Faun!



آ آ آ آ IV

آ آ آ آ APOLLO

آ آ آ آ CALLICLES (front below)

آ آ آ آ Through the black, rushing smoke-bursts,
آ آ آ آ Thick breaks the red flame;
آ آ آ آ All Etna heaves fiercely
آ آ آ آ Her forest-clothï؟½d frame.

آ آ آ آ Not here, O Apollo
آ آ آ آ Are haunts meet for thee.
آ آ آ آ But, where Helicon breaks down
آ آ آ آ In cliff to the sea,

آ آ آ آ Where the moon-silverï؟½d inlets
آ آ آ آ Send far their light voice
آ آ آ آ Up the still vale of Thisbe,
آ آ آ آ O speed, and rejoice!

آ آ آ آ On the sward at the cliff-top
آ آ آ آ Lie strewn the white flocks;
آ آ آ آ On the cliff-side the pigeons
آ آ آ آ Roost deep in the rocks.

آ آ آ آ In the moonlight the shepherds,
آ آ آ آ Soft lullï؟½d by the rills,
آ آ آ آ Lie wrapt in their blankets,
آ آ آ آ Asleep on the hills.

آ آ آ آ What forms are these coming
آ آ آ آ So white through the gloom:ï؟½
آ آ آ آ What garments out-glistening
آ آ آ آ The gold-flowerï؟½d broom?

آ آ آ آ What sweet-breathing presence
آ آ آ آ Out-perfumes the thyme?
آ آ آ آ What voices enrapture
آ آ آ آ The nightï؟½s balmy prime?

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis Apollo comes leading
آ آ آ آ His choir, the Nine.
آ آ آ آ The leader is fairest,
آ آ آ آ But all are divine.

آ آ آ آ They are lost in the hollows!
آ آ آ آ They stream up again!
آ آ آ آ What seeks on this mountain
آ آ آ آ The glorified train?

آ آ آ آ They bathe on this mountain,
آ آ آ آ In the spring by their road;
آ آ آ آ Then on to Olympus,
آ آ آ آ Their endless abode!

آ آ آ آ Whose praise do they mention
آ آ آ آ Of what is it told?
آ آ آ آ What will be for ever;
آ آ آ آ What was from of old.

آ آ آ آ First hymn they the Father
آ آ آ آ Of all things; and then
آ آ آ آ The rest of immortals,
آ آ آ آ The action of men.

آ آ آ آ The day in his hotness,
آ آ آ آ The strife with the palm;
آ آ آ آ The night in her silence,
آ آ آ آ The stars in their calm.
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
